citations.desc: Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors).
citations.narrative:
The facility failed to notify the attending veterinarian after a female prairie dog stopped eating on Friday April 3rd and
subsequently died the next day. An employee reported the animal’s condition to the licensee via text message on the
morning of Saturday April 4th. The licensee texted the employee stating she texted the attending veterinarian. However,
after talking to the attending veterinarian, the attending veterinarian stated that the licensee knows she does not offer
emergency care on the weekends. The animal died later that day on April 4th.
Effective and timely communication between licensees, their employees, and the attending veterinarian is essential to
safeguarding the health and welfare of all animals. Prompt updates and collaboration can prevent problems from
escalating—many illnesses can be addressed quickly and effectively with immediate notification to the veterinarian. All
licensees are required to maintain veterinary coverage at all times, including weekends and holidays.
Each dealer or exhibitor shall establish and maintain programs of adequate veterinary care that include the use of
appropriate methods to prevent, control, diagnose, and treat diseases and injuries, and the availability of emergency,
weekend, and holiday care. To be corrected this day, April 30, 2026, forward.

citations.desc: Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors).
citations.narrative:
The facility failed to identify and address an eye lesion in an adult Crab-eating male raccoon. During a routine inspection,
USDA inspectors observed an approximately 6mm circular lesion on the animal’s right eye. The lesion appeared cloudy
and blue in color, with a central indentation. Inspectors also observed cloudiness in both eyes. When questioned, the
facility representative stated that this condition was “normal for him” because of his cataracts. Inspectors consulted with
the attending veterinarian, who agreed it appeared to be a new corneal lesion. The attending veterinarian also stated that
the animal has a history of cataracts in both eyes and was treated for a similar corneal lesion in 2023 but had not
undergone any treatment or evaluation since that time.
Failure to closely observe animals on a daily basis may result in significant behavioral or physical changes going
unnoticed. This can delay the identification of illness or injury, leading to prolonged discomfort and inadequate or untimely
veterinary care. Daily and attentive observation of all animals is essential to identify early indicators of illness, injury, or
distress.
Each dealer or exhibitor shall establish and maintain programs of adequate veterinary care that include daily observation
of all animals to assess their health and well-being. To be corrected this day, April 30, 2026, forward.

There is a build up of dried fecal matter on a tire swing in an enclosure housing 7 black-and-white ruffed lemurs.
Failing to maintain clean conditions in areas accessible to nonhuman primates can lead to significant accumulations of
debris and contaminants, increasing the risk of disease and compromising the health and welfare of the animals housed
there in.
Hard surfaces with which nonhuman primates come in contact must be spot cleaned daily and sanitized in accordance
with 3.84 of this subpart to prevent accumulation of excreta or disease hazards. Contaminated material must be removed
or replaced whenever raking and spot cleaning does not eliminate odors, diseases, insects, pests, or vermin infestation.
Correct by May 1, 2026.

Food used to feed non-human primates was not stored properly. Apples, carrots, squash, and corn were stored in the
door of the refrigerator without being placed in a leakproof container with a tightly fitting lid. The inside walls and shelves
of the refrigerator were dirty and spattered with food material. The seals were dirty and contained mold in some areas.
There was a colony of ants seen marching across the top of the refrigerator door.
Improper food storage can reduce nutritional value and palatability. Open food items must be kept in leakproof, tightly
sealed containers to prevent spoilage, contamination, and pest infestation.
Food requiring refrigeration must be stored accordingly, and all food must be stored in a manner that prevents
contamination and deterioration of its nutritive value. Open food and bedding supplies must be kept in leakproof
containers with tightly fitting lids to prevent spoilage and contamination.

citations.desc: Cleaning, sanitization, housekeeping, and pest control.
citations.narrative:
The facility does not currently maintain an effective pest control program. There is a large quantity of rodent droppings on
bookshelves and throughout the room housing a black and white ruffed lemur and in the garage that acts as the main
food prep and storage area for the facility. While the facility does set traps, it is not effective in managing the rodent
population.
Pests such as rodents and insects can introduce pathogens, parasites, and environmental contaminants that pose direct
health risks to animals. Contaminated feed or water can also diminish animal welfare, reduce nutritional quality, and
create stressful or unsafe living conditions.
An effective program for control of insects, external parasites affecting nonhuman primates, and birds and mammals that
are pests, must be established and maintained so as to promote the health and well- being of the animals and reduce
contamination by pests in animal areas.

The metal gates housing pigs are in disrepair. Two metal gates housing a total of three pigs in two separate enclosures
are eaten away by rust on the bottom bar, leaving sharp, ragged edges.
Rusted, sharp metal can easily cause cuts or puncture wounds to people or animals passing through or working near the
gate. It also weakens the gate’s structural integrity, making it more likely to bend, break, or fail under normal use which
may lead to animal escape.
The facility must be constructed of such material and of such strength as appropriate for the animals involved. The indoor
and outdoor housing facilities shall be structurally sound and shall be maintained in good repair to protect the animals
from injury and to contain the animals.

There is a wooden pallet in disrepair in a duck’s enclosure. A wooden pallet elevating a swimming pool in an enclosure
containing one duck has broken boards with sharp edges.
Broken boards are dangerous because they create sharp edges, splinters, and unstable surfaces that can easily injure a
bird. Even a small fracture in a perch, platform, or crate can lead to cuts or punctures, foot and leg injuries, snags or
entanglement, stress or behavioral changes as the animal tries to avoid the unsafe area.
Furniture-type objects, such as perches and other objects that enrich a bird’s environment, must be species-appropriate
and be designed, constructed, and maintained so as to prevent harm to the bird. Correct by May 1, 2026.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the facility representative.
Additional Inspectors:
Colette Kirk El-Amin, VETERINARY MEDICAL OFFICER n
